**Roc-A-Fella Records** was an American [record label](/wiki/Record_label "Record label") and music management company founded in 1994 by record executives and entrepreneurs [Damon Dash](/wiki/Damon_Dash "Damon Dash"), [Shawn "Jay-Z" Carter](/wiki/Jay-Z "Jay-Z"), and [Kareem "Biggs" Burke](/wiki/Kareem_Burke "Kareem Burke").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Carter issued his debut album, *[Reasonable Doubt](/wiki/Reasonable_Doubt_(album) "Reasonable Doubt (album)")* (1996), as the label's first release, in a joint venture with [Priority Records](/wiki/Priority_Records "Priority Records"). The label signed and released albums for acts including [Kanye West](/wiki/Kanye_West "Kanye West"), [Memphis Bleek](/wiki/Memphis_Bleek "Memphis Bleek"), [Jadakiss](/wiki/Jadakiss "Jadakiss"), [DJ Clue?](/wiki/DJ_Clue "DJ Clue"), [State Property](/wiki/State_Property_(group) "State Property (group)"), and [The Diplomats](/wiki/The_Diplomats "The Diplomats") before its dissolution in 2013.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Roc-A-Fella_Records&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1994–2000: Formation and early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Roc-A-Fella_Records&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1994–2000: Formation and early years")]

Roc-A-Fella Records was founded in 1994.[[4]](#cite_note-4) After [Shawn "Jay-Z" Carter](/wiki/Jay-Z "Jay-Z") had been rejected by major record labels, it began as an independent outlet for the rapper's debut album. Carter, [Damon Dash](/wiki/Damon_Dash "Damon Dash") and [Kareem Burke](/wiki/Kareem_Burke "Kareem Burke") started the label through [Priority Records](/wiki/Priority_Records "Priority Records"), using finances from [Payday Records](/wiki/Payday_Records "Payday Records") due to their singles-only deal.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) The name "Roc-A-Fella" was conceived by rapper [Tone Hooker](/wiki/Original_Flavor "Original Flavor") as a double entendre for [Rockefeller](/wiki/John_D._Rockefeller "John D. Rockefeller") and a rapper dominating his opponent ("rocking a fella").[[7]](#cite_note-7) Though *[Reasonable Doubt](/wiki/Reasonable_Doubt_(album) "Reasonable Doubt (album)")* (1996) didn't earn immediate commercial success, it spawned successful singles and earned Jay-Z regional popularity in hip hop. Starting out as Roc-A-Fella's only artist, Jay-Z was supported by [the Notorious B.I.G.](/wiki/The_Notorious_B.I.G. "The Notorious B.I.G.")'s producer [DJ Clark Kent](/wiki/Clark_Kent_(producer) "Clark Kent (producer)") and [Camp Lo](/wiki/Camp_Lo "Camp Lo")'s producer [DJ Ski](/wiki/Ski_Beatz "Ski Beatz"); affiliated rappers, [Sauce Money](/wiki/Sauce_Money "Sauce Money"), [Jaz-O](/wiki/Jaz-O "Jaz-O"), and a young [Memphis Bleek](/wiki/Memphis_Bleek "Memphis Bleek"), though only Memphis Bleek would eventually sign with the label.

In June 1997, Roc-A-Fella agreed to a 50/50 partnership and distribution deal with [Def Jam Recordings](/wiki/Def_Jam_Recordings "Def Jam Recordings"). In November 1997, Roc-A-Fella released their first two albums under Def Jam's distribution, Jay-Z's sophomore album, *[In My Lifetime, Vol. 1](/wiki/In_My_Lifetime,_Vol._1 "In My Lifetime, Vol. 1")*, as well as R&B duo [Christión](/wiki/Christi%C3%B3n "Christión")'s debut and only album for the label, *[Ghetto Cyrano](/wiki/Ghetto_Cyrano "Ghetto Cyrano")*, on the same day. Roc-A-Fella and Jay-Z saw increasing popularity, mainly due to a high-profile appearance by Jay on The Notorious B.I.G.'s posthumous *[Life After Death](/wiki/Life_After_Death_(album) "Life After Death (album)")*, complete with Roc-A-Fella and Damon Dash references. While Memphis Bleek signed with Roc-A-Fella, Sauce Money chose to pursue a deal with Priority, and Jaz-O refrained from signing anywhere and provided production for only one song on *In My Lifetime, Vol. 1*, "Rap Game/Crack Game." Despite Jaz-O not signing to Roc-A-Fella, he became Memphis Bleek's manager. In 1998, Roc-A-Fella Records released the movie *[Streets Is Watching](/wiki/Streets_Is_Watching_(film) "Streets Is Watching (film)")* and the accompanying [soundtrack](/wiki/Streets_Is_Watching_(soundtrack) "Streets Is Watching (soundtrack)"); the film compiles various Jay-Z videos into a continuous story, and the album introduced more affiliated, future Roc-A-Fella Records acts like [Noreaga](/wiki/Noreaga "Noreaga"), [M.O.P.](/wiki/M.O.P. "M.O.P."), and [DJ Clue](/wiki/DJ_Clue "DJ Clue"), as well as producer [Irv Gotti](/wiki/Irv_Gotti "Irv Gotti") and the short-lived group, [Murder Inc.](/wiki/Murder_Inc._(rap_group) "Murder Inc. (rap group)") (namesake of Irv's record label, [Murder Inc.](/wiki/Murder_Inc._Records "Murder Inc. Records")).

Jay's 1998 album, *[Vol. 2... Hard Knock Life](/wiki/Vol._2..._Hard_Knock_Life "Vol. 2... Hard Knock Life")*, saw him largely depart from his previous entourage and venture forth with producers [Swizz Beatz](/wiki/Swizz_Beatz "Swizz Beatz"), [Timbaland](/wiki/Timbaland "Timbaland"), [The 45 King](/wiki/The_45_King "The 45 King") and [Jermaine Dupri](/wiki/Jermaine_Dupri "Jermaine Dupri"). *Vol. 2* spawned his first major hit, "[Hard Knock Life](/wiki/Hard_Knock_Life_(Ghetto_Anthem) "Hard Knock Life (Ghetto Anthem)")", and became the label's first Platinum-RIAA certified release; it was the last Roc-A-Fella release to see appearances by Jaz-O or Sauce Money, and the first to feature new Roc artists [Beanie Sigel](/wiki/Beanie_Sigel "Beanie Sigel") and [Amil](/wiki/Amil "Amil"). The label closed out 1998 with the December release of DJ Clue's debut album, *[The Professional](/wiki/The_Professional_(album) "The Professional (album)")*, which saw guest appearances from Jay-Z, Memphis Bleek, and an appearance from future Roc-A-Fella artist [Cam'ron](/wiki/Cam%27ron "Cam'ron"). The album was certified platinum by the RIAA, making DJ Clue the first other than Jay-Z to achieve platinum status. Meanwhile, DJ Ski had, at the time, formed the production company Roc-A-Blok, although the company folded when Ski moved out of New York to take a break from music.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In August 1999, Memphis Bleek released his debut album *[Coming of Age](/wiki/Coming_of_Age_(Memphis_Bleek_album) "Coming of Age (Memphis Bleek album)")* through Roc-A-Fella. The album was executive produced by Kareem "Biggs" Burke, Damon Dash, and Jay-Z, and featured guest appearances from Roc-A-Fella artists Beanie Sigel and Jay-Z, as well as Dark Half and Da Ranjahz, who were signed to the label's subsidiary, The Carter Foundation. Despite their contributions, both Dark Half and Da Ranjahz eventually parted ways with The Carter Foundation. While Coming of Age didn’t reach the same level of success as Jay-Z’s albums, it achieved Gold status in the U.S., driven by the single "What You Think of That" featuring Jay-Z. Jay-Z's 1999 album *[Vol. 3... Life and Times of S. Carter](/wiki/Vol._3..._Life_and_Times_of_S._Carter "Vol. 3... Life and Times of S. Carter")* continued Jay's new affiliations with then-popular producers; in 2000, the label saw a redefinition in both sound and roster. Jay-Z put out *[The Dynasty: Roc La Familia](/wiki/The_Dynasty:_Roc_La_Familia "The Dynasty: Roc La Familia")* as a solo album. Originally intended to be a collaboration project, it nonetheless featured heavy appearances by Beanie Sigel, Amil, and Memphis Bleek, along with a Philly rapper [Freeway](/wiki/Freeway_(rapper) "Freeway (rapper)") guest spot that led to him being signed to Roc-A-Fella. Rather than return to Timbaland or Swizz Beatz for production, Jay selected beats from a new crop of producers: [Kanye West](/wiki/Kanye_West "Kanye West"), [Bink](/wiki/Bink_(record_producer) "Bink (record producer)"), [The Neptunes](/wiki/The_Neptunes "The Neptunes") and [Just Blaze](/wiki/Just_Blaze "Just Blaze"). Each beat-smith would go on to become consistently involved in future Roc-A-Fella projects.

### 2000–2005: Prominence and split

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Roc-A-Fella_Records&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2000–2005: Prominence and split")]

The new decade saw Roc-A-Fella begin to expand beyond one figurehead artist. Although Jay-Z remained the label's prominent image—with the acclaimed[[9]](#cite_note-9) release of *[The Blueprint](/wiki/The_Blueprint_(album) "The Blueprint (album)")* and the closing of his trial for the 1999 stabbing of producer [Lance Rivera](/wiki/Lance_Rivera "Lance Rivera")[[10]](#cite_note-10)—other Roc artists began to gain popularity and acceptance. In 2000, Beanie Sigel released *[The Truth](/wiki/The_Truth_(Beanie_Sigel_album) "The Truth (Beanie Sigel album)")* and reached #5 on the Billboard charts, DJ Clue released *[The Professional 2](/wiki/The_Professional_2 "The Professional 2")*, and Memphis Bleek released *[The Understanding](/wiki/The_Understanding_(Memphis_Bleek_album) "The Understanding (Memphis Bleek album)")*. Although Clue and Beans's albums hit the top five on the Billboard charts, Bleek's album was in the top twenty. Nonetheless, all three albums were certified Gold by the RIAA. Amil's album, however, had lackluster sales. Jay-Z and Damon Dash began signing up new talent, including Freeway, and several young Philadelphia rappers that were later compiled into the Freeway/Sigel-led group, [State Property](/wiki/State_Property_(band) "State Property (band)"). During this time, Jay-Z and Beanie Sigel were embroiled in a feud with [Ruff Ryders](/wiki/Ruff_Ryders_Entertainment "Ruff Ryders Entertainment") artists [Jadakiss](/wiki/Jadakiss "Jadakiss") and [DMX](/wiki/DMX "DMX"). Disses back and forth between Jay-Z and Jadakiss implied a conflict between Jay and former groupmate DMX, led to a full-on war of words between Sigel and Kiss, and eventually culminated in a diss by Beanie Sigel over Jada's hit "Put Your Hands Up," after which the rivalry faded.[[11]](#cite_note-11) On December 4, 2001 (Jay-Z's 32nd birthday), it was announced and confirmed that rapper [Cam'ron](/wiki/Cam%27ron "Cam'ron") had signed to Roc-A-Fella through his connection with [Damon Dash](/wiki/Damon_Dash "Damon Dash"). After Cam'ron was signed, his rap group [The Diplomats](/wiki/The_Diplomats "The Diplomats") as well as Diplomats members [Juelz Santana](/wiki/Juelz_Santana "Juelz Santana") and [Jimmy Jones](/wiki/Jim_Jones_(rapper) "Jim Jones (rapper)") also signed to Roc-A-Fella as well.

In January 2002, Roc-A-Fella released the film, *[State Property](/wiki/State_Property_(film) "State Property (film)")*. It starred Roc-A-Fella acts Damon Dash, Jay-Z, Beanie Sigel, Rell, Memphis Bleek, and DJ Clue. The title of became the namesake for both the Philadelphia based [State Property](/wiki/State_Property_(group) "State Property (group)") and Rocawear's subsidiary clothing line. Upon signing to Roc-A-Fella, Cam'ron reached a new commercial peak. In May, Cam'ron released his Roc-A-Fella debut *[Come Home With Me](/wiki/Come_Home_With_Me "Come Home With Me")*. The album featured guest appearances from Roc-A-Fella artists Jimmy Jones, Juelz Santana, Beanie Sigel, Memphis Bleek, and Jay-Z, and featured production from Roc-A-Fella's in house producers Just Blaze and [Kanye West](/wiki/Kanye_West "Kanye West"). It featured two hit singles "[Oh Boy](/wiki/Oh_Boy_(Cam%27ron_song) "Oh Boy (Cam'ron song)")" and "[Hey Ma](/wiki/Hey_Ma_(Cam%27ron_song) "Hey Ma (Cam'ron song)")" (both featuring Juelz Santana), which both peaked in the top 5 on the Billboard Hot 100, as well as the moderately successful third single "Daydreaming". The album was certified Platinum by the RIAA for selling over a million copies in America. From 2002 to 2003, Damon Dash signed several artists in response to Jay-Z's talk of retirement after his 2002 album *[The Blueprint2: The Gift & The Curse](/wiki/The_Blueprint_2:_The_Gift_%26_The_Curse "The Blueprint 2: The Gift & The Curse")*. He signed [M.O.P.](/wiki/M.O.P. "M.O.P.") and [Ol' Dirty Bastard](/wiki/Ol%27_Dirty_Bastard "Ol' Dirty Bastard") and attempted to sign [Twista](/wiki/Twista "Twista") and [Joe Budden](/wiki/Joe_Budden "Joe Budden"). Twista never signed to Roc-A-Fella due to his deal with [Atlantic Records](/wiki/Atlantic_Records "Atlantic Records"). Roc-A-Fella experienced its height in product releases and overall popularity as a brand name during this period. On March 25, 2003, Roc-A-Fella released The Diplomats' debut album *[Diplomatic Immunity](/wiki/Diplomatic_Immunity_(The_Diplomats_album) "Diplomatic Immunity (The Diplomats album)")*. Despite not producing any major hits, the success of the singles *Built This City* and *Dipset Anthem* led to the album being certified Gold by the RIAA. In late 2003, the company released Jay-Z's alleged final album, *[The Black Album](/wiki/The_Black_Album_(Jay-Z_album) "The Black Album (Jay-Z album)")*.

In January 2004, Jay-Z entered talks with Island Def Jam to launch his own entertainment company, separate from Roc-A-Fella. The move sparked speculation about friction between him and Dash, fueled by concerns over Dash’s growing media presence and his push to expand Roc-A-Fella into film—a direction Jay-Z reportedly opposed. However, both parties publicly denied any conflict at the time.[[12]](#cite_note-NYPost2004-12) After Jay-Z's supposed last hurrah, it was announced and confirmed in December 2004 that [The Island Def Jam Music Group](/wiki/The_Island_Def_Jam_Music_Group "The Island Def Jam Music Group") purchased the remaining 50 percent stake of Roc-A-Fella Records that IDJ didn't already own, and named Jay-Z as president/CEO of Def Jam.[[13]](#cite_note-BillboardIDJ-13) Dash, poised to take greater control in the company, began heavily promoting artists Cam'ron, The Diplomats, State Property, Kanye West, and Twista. In 2004, Kanye West's album, *[The College Dropout](/wiki/The_College_Dropout "The College Dropout")*, became a huge commercial and critical success, selling multi-Platinum-RIAA certified sales, and [Foxy Brown](/wiki/Foxy_Brown_(rapper) "Foxy Brown (rapper)") was signed and began work on her album, *Black Rose*. The infamous 'split' between Dash, Carter, and Burke occurred when it was revealed the trio had sold their 50% interest in Roc-A-Fella to The Island Def Jam Music Group, making the label full owners. As President, Carter retained control of the Roc and his masters, ousting his 2 former partners. He later explained that he had offered to turn down the position and ownership for the masters to *Reasonable Doubt* alone:

> So I was like, let me get *Reasonable Doubt* and I'll give up [the rest of] my masters. I'll give up Roc-A-Fella, I'll give up president and CEO of Def Jam—everything. Just give me my baby to hold on to so 10 years down the line, I can look back and I got something—I'm not empty-handed. And I was the 1 being offered everything. I thought it was more than fair ... And when that was turned down, I had to make a choice. I'll leave that for the people to say what choice they would've made. That's about it. I don't really wanna talk about Dame or Biggs. I don't have nothing negative to say about them.

— Jay-Z, XXL

As Dash and Burke set up their own fledgling record label, originally called *Roc4life* and later rechristened to *Dame Dash Music Group*, each artist was offered their choice of labels. Memphis Bleek and Kanye West remained on Roc-A-Fella. Jimmy Jones and The Diplomats opted to leave Roc-A-Fella in 2004.

Beanie Sigel, then doing a year's incarceration on an attempted murder charge, put out his album *[The B. Coming](/wiki/The_B._Coming "The B. Coming")* on Dame Dash and Roc-A-Fella; this was accompanied by accusations from Dash that of all the members of State Property, only Oschino had gone to visit Sigel in prison. Beanie had initially chosen Dame Dash Music Group, and he wanted to bring the rest of State Property over to Dame Dash. However, the rest of the group refused, preferring to remain on Roc-A-Fella; in response, Beanie Sigel effectively put the group on hold, claiming disappointment in his groupmates. M.O.P. also left Roc-A-Fella for Dame Dash Music Group, though they parted ways with Dash soon thereafter. Due to the 2004 death of Ol' Dirty Bastard, Dash also brought with him masters of the rapper's project and promises to release the album, *[A Son Unique](/wiki/A_Son_Unique "A Son Unique")*, though this never occurred. On April 28, 2005, Cam'ron left Roc-A-Fella and Def Jam and signed to [Warner Music Group](/wiki/Warner_Music_Group "Warner Music Group") under the Asylum Records imprint.[[14]](#cite_note-asylum-14) Cam'ron was especially vocal in running a smear campaign against Jay-Z, claiming Jay-Z blocked him from an executive position Dash had offered him at Roc-A-Fella.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Memphis Bleek and Kanye West released *[534](/wiki/534_(album) "534 (album)")* and *[Late Registration](/wiki/Late_Registration "Late Registration")*, respectively, in 2005, along with the [Young Gunz](/wiki/Young_Gunz "Young Gunz")' sophomore effort and [Teairra Marí](/wiki/Teairra_Mar%C3%AD "Teairra Marí")'s debut, though only Kanye West's project saw significant sales. It was stated by Memphis Bleek that [Cory Gunz](/wiki/Cory_Gunz "Cory Gunz") had signed, but nothing materialized. In May 2005, the Damon Dash Music Group's deal with Island Def Jam collapsed just three months after its launch. The split came amid Dash’s demands for increased marketing funds and a larger executive role, which strained relations with Def Jam executives. Additionally, Dash grew tired of Jay-Z, now president of Def Jam, overseeing his projects. Dame Dash Music Group subsequently left Def Jam and by 2007 was dissolved.[[16]](#cite_note-NYPost2005-16)

### 2006–2009: Roc Redefinition and departure of Jay-Z

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Roc-A-Fella_Records&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2006–2009: Roc Redefinition and departure of Jay-Z")]

In 2006, releases were largely limited to those of [Roc-La-Familia](/wiki/Roc-La-Familia "Roc-La-Familia"), a Latino-geared label under Roc-A-Fella that followed the trend of [reggaeton](/wiki/Reggaeton "Reggaeton"). [Héctor el Father](/wiki/H%C3%A9ctor_el_Father "Héctor el Father") and [N.O.R.E.](/wiki/N.O.R.E. "N.O.R.E.") both put out albums, and the label was home to New York rapper [Tru Life](/wiki/Tru_Life "Tru Life"), but has since folded. Jay-Z made his return that year with *[Kingdom Come](/wiki/Kingdom_Come_(Jay-Z_album) "Kingdom Come (Jay-Z album)")*, to mixed reviews. He stepped down from his Def Jam position and put out a second album in 2007, *[American Gangster](/wiki/American_Gangster_(album) "American Gangster (album)")*, to more positive reviews and sales, along with Kanye West's *[Graduation](/wiki/Graduation_(album) "Graduation (album)")*, Beanie Sigel's *[The Solution](/wiki/The_Solution_(Beanie_Sigel_album) "The Solution (Beanie Sigel album)")*, and Freeway's *[Free at Last](/wiki/Free_at_Last_(Freeway_album) "Free at Last (Freeway album)")*; Kanye West's album sold multi-platinum to rave reviews. Freeway's project received acclaim but not major sales, and contained comments aimed at Kanye West and Just Blaze for not supplying production. He later amended his comments, stating he desired to work with Just Blaze but the producer hasn't reached out. This may have been due to Just Blaze's work on *American Gangster* and complications regarding his [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Records "Atlantic Records")-distributed label, [Fort Knocks](/wiki/Fort_Knocks_Entertainment "Fort Knocks Entertainment"), and his artist [Saigon](/wiki/Saigon_(rapper) "Saigon (rapper)").

The signing of Ruff Ryders artist Jadakiss, former rival to both Jay-Z and Beanie Sigel, also came in 2007, as did [Uncle Murda](/wiki/Uncle_Murda "Uncle Murda"). Foxy Brown was dropped from the label after 2 years, in light of a jail sentence. Though Young Chris and Peedi Crakk continued to appear on projects, neither seemed any closer to solo projects, and in 2008 Peedi Crakk announced that State Property had been dropped from the label.[[17]](#cite_note-17) This was countered by Beanie Sigel's manager, who confirmed that Beanie Sigel and Freeway were still part of Roc-A-Fella, though Beanie Sigel would later leave that year. Young Chris also apparently signed as a solo artist. 2008 saw only the release of Kanye West's *[808s & Heartbreak](/wiki/808s_%26_Heartbreak "808s & Heartbreak")*, garnering decent sales. It also brought repeated disses in songs and interviews from Peedi Crakk towards Jay-Z, claiming he held up his project on purpose, though he claims to have moved on. During that year, Jay-Z had signed a $150 million deal with [Live Nation](/wiki/Live_Nation_Entertainment "Live Nation Entertainment") that included concerts, endorsements and recordings, and included a platform for him to launch his [Roc Nation](/wiki/Roc_Nation "Roc Nation") label.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Uncle Murda left the label after a year and a half with no release, citing lack of executive interest after Jay-Z left Def Jam.

In March 2009, Freeway procured his release from Def Jam, claiming a need to explore his options; shortly, he announced his signing to [Ca$h Money](/wiki/Cash_Money_Records "Cash Money Records"), while stating he would always respect Roc-A-Fella.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Longtime signee Memphis Bleek also reported his departure from Def Jam, deciding not to travel to Roc Nation in favor of starting his own record label, but he is still very close with Roc-A-Fella.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Additionally, Tru Life has been referred to as a "1 time [or past] affiliate" of Roc-A-Fella upon his turning himself in to authorities for his connection to a retaliatory stabbing.[[21]](#cite_note-21) On May 21, 2009, Jay-Z had bought back his contract from Def Jam for an unprecedented $5,000,000 and started his deal with Live Nation.

### 2010–2013: Final years and TufAmerica vs. Roc-A-Fella

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Roc-A-Fella_Records&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2010–2013: Final years and TufAmerica vs. Roc-A-Fella")]

Jadakiss briefly moved to Roc-A-Fella and released an album *[The Last Kiss](/wiki/The_Last_Kiss_(album) "The Last Kiss (album)")* before reuniting with Ruff Ryders.[[22]](#cite_note-22) On May 3, 2010, Damon Dash relaunched Roc-A-Fella after nearly a year of inactivity with his 1st artist being former [Young Money](/wiki/Young_Money_Entertainment "Young Money Entertainment") rapper [Curren$y](/wiki/Currensy "Currensy").[[23]](#cite_note-relaunch-23) Curren$y's third album, *[Pilot Talk](/wiki/Pilot_Talk "Pilot Talk")*, was to be released under the newly relaunched Roc-A-Fella.[[24]](#cite_note-24) However, Curren$y stated in interviews with both *[XXL](/wiki/XXL_(magazine) "XXL (magazine)")* and *[Complex](/wiki/Complex_Networks "Complex Networks")* that the album would be released under [Damon Dash](/wiki/Damon_Dash "Damon Dash")'s [DD172](/wiki/DD172 "DD172") record label division, BluRoc Records[[25]](#cite_note-ddotomen1-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) and distributed through Def Jam. On August 8, 2011, Jay-Z and Kanye West released a collaborative album titled *[Watch the Throne](/wiki/Watch_the_Throne "Watch the Throne")*. It was later revealed that Jay-Z was part of a short-lived relaunched Roc-A-Fella, as the album was released on Roc-A-Fella, Roc Nation, and Def Jam.

In September 2012, record label [Tuff City](/wiki/Tuff_City_Records "Tuff City Records"), via its subsidiary TufAmerica, filed a lawsuit on the grounds of copyright infringement by Roc-A-Fella, seeking undisclosed damages.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The complaint filed in [federal court](/wiki/United_States_district_court "United States district court") in Manhattan claims Roc-A-Fella and their parent [Island Def Jam](/wiki/The_Island_Def_Jam_Music_Group "The Island Def Jam Music Group") paid them a $62,500 [license](/wiki/License "License") fee to sample [Eddie Bo](/wiki/Eddie_Bo "Eddie Bo")'s "Hook and Sling, Part 1" in [Kanye West](/wiki/Kanye_West "Kanye West")'s "Who Will Survive in America" and "[Lost in the World](/wiki/Lost_in_the_World "Lost in the World")". Despite this, Tuff City says UMG and Roc-A-Fella "failed and refused to enter into written license agreements that accounted for their multiple other uses of 'Hook and Sling'".[[29]](#cite_note-29) The unmentioned "multiple other" uses TufAmerica refers to are in the "[Lost in the World](/wiki/Lost_in_the_World#Music_video "Lost in the World")" video and [the short film](/wiki/Runaway_(2010_film) "Runaway (2010 film)") based on Kanye's "[Runaway](/wiki/Runaway_(Kanye_West_song) "Runaway (Kanye West song)")". Tuff City is represented by New York attorney Kelly Talcott.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

On June 16, 2013, Jay-Z announced with a tweet: "VII IV XIII Roc A Fella/Roc Nation", hinting a possible relaunch and revival of Roc-A-Fella and a possible merger with Roc Nation. However, it was later revealed that the tweet instead meant that Roc-A-Fella was only temporarily relaunched, specifically for the sole purpose of releasing his new album, *[Magna Carta Holy Grail](/wiki/Magna_Carta_Holy_Grail "Magna Carta Holy Grail")*.

## Legal issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Roc-A-Fella_Records&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Legal issues")]

In June 2021, Roc-A-Fella sued its co-founder Damon Dash for attempting to auction off a [non-fungible token](/wiki/Non-fungible_token "Non-fungible token") representing the Jay-Z album *[Reasonable Doubt](/wiki/Reasonable_Doubt_(album) "Reasonable Doubt (album)")*, due to Roc-A-Fella owning the rights to the album. In response, Dash claimed that he was not trying to sell the album, but his own share of the record label. On June 22, a restraining order prohibiting Dash from further selling the album was placed.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Subsidiaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Roc-A-Fella_Records&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Subsidiaries")]

### ROC Films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Roc-A-Fella_Records&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: ROC Films")]

In 2002, Roc-A-Fella released, through Lions Gate Entertainment, *[State Property](/wiki/State_Property_(film) "State Property (film)")*. The movie, while not the first for Dash, would be the start of ROC Films/Roc-A-Fella Films. It starred Roc-A-Fella acts Damon Dash, Jay-Z, Beanie Sigel, DJ Clue, Memphis Bleek, and Rell. The film studio would go on to release *[Paid in Full](/wiki/Paid_in_Full_(2002_film) "Paid in Full (2002 film)")* the same year, and follow up in 2003 with *[Paper Soldiers](/wiki/Paper_Soldiers "Paper Soldiers")* and the hip-hop satire *[Death of a Dynasty](/wiki/Death_of_a_Dynasty "Death of a Dynasty")*. In 2005, the sequel to the studio's debut release would hit theatres, *[State Property 2](/wiki/State_Property_2 "State Property 2")*, featuring rap stars such as Beanie Sigel and Damon Dash, who also produced and co-created the story. Cameo roles included musicians Kanye West, N.O.R.E, and Mariah Carey, and light heavyweight champion boxers Bernard Hopkins and Winky Wright. The list of films include:

* *[Streets Is Watching](/wiki/Streets_Is_Watching_(film) "Streets Is Watching (film)")* (1998)
* *[Backstage](/wiki/Backstage_(2000_film) "Backstage (2000 film)")* (2000)
* *[State Property](/wiki/State_Property_(film) "State Property (film)")* (2002)
* *[Paid in Full](/wiki/Paid_in_Full_(2002_film) "Paid in Full (2002 film)")* (2002)
* *[Paper Soldiers](/wiki/Paper_Soldiers "Paper Soldiers")* (2002)
* *[Death of a Dynasty](/wiki/Death_of_a_Dynasty "Death of a Dynasty")* (2003)
* *[Fade to Black](/wiki/Fade_to_Black_(2004_film) "Fade to Black (2004 film)")* (2004)
* *[State Property 2](/wiki/State_Property_2 "State Property 2")* (2005)

### Roc La Familia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Roc-A-Fella_Records&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Roc La Familia")]

Roc-La-Familia was founded in 2005 by then Roc-A-Fella and [Def Jam](/wiki/Def_Jam_Recordings "Def Jam Recordings") President/CEO [Shawn Carter](/wiki/Jay-Z "Jay-Z"). This sub-label was created to focus on signing international recording artists.
